CD2_1.relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

CD2_2.content: This value is an element of the coordinate description matrix, which defines how pixel coordinates are transformed into world coordinates (like Right Ascension and Declination). It specifically contributes to scaling, rotation, and skew of the image when mapping it to the sky. While not directly viewed by most users, it is crucial for software to accurately display the image with correct celestial coordinates.
CD2_2.relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

PC1_1.content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
PC1_1.relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

PC1_2.content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
PC1_2.relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

PC2_1.content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
PC2_1.relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

PC2_2.content: This value is an element of the projection coordinate (PC) matrix, an alternative or complementary way to define the linear transformation from pixel coordinates to celestial coordinates. Like the CDi_j matrix, it encodes information about the pixel scale, rotation, and any skew present in the image. It is fundamental for software to correctly interpret the image's orientation and scale on the sky.
PC2_2.relevance_comment: These values are critical for the software to accurately transform pixel locations into celestial coordinates, enabling precise astrometry and sky overlays.

RADESYS.content: This keyword specifies the celestial coordinate reference system (e.g., ICRS, FK5) used for the Right Ascension and Declination values in the data. Knowing the reference system is vital for accurately interpreting object positions, comparing data from different sources, and ensuring proper coordinate transformations. For example, positions in ICRS are fixed, while FK5 positions slowly change due to precession.
RADESYS.relevance_comment: This keyword is essential for understanding the specific celestial coordinate system used for the data's RA/Dec values, ensuring correct interpretation and comparisons.

EQUINOX.content: This keyword indicates the equinox (epoch) of the celestial coordinate system used for the data's Right Ascension and Declination, most commonly J2000.0. The equinox defines the orientation of the coordinate axes at a specific point in time, which is crucial for precise astrometry. Comparing observations from different eras requires knowing their respective equinoxes to account for precession.
EQUINOX.relevance_comment: This keyword specifies the epoch of the celestial coordinate system, crucial for accurate astrometry and for comparing observations across different periods, especially if not J2000.0.

LONPOLE.content: This keyword specifies the longitude of the native celestial pole in degrees, which is a technical parameter used in certain World Coordinate System (WCS) projections. It helps define the orientation of the projection, particularly when transforming to or from non-standard coordinate systems or projections. Most end-users will not directly interact with this value, but it is used by WCS software for accurate sky mapping.
LONPOLE.relevance_comment: This keyword is a technical detail within the WCS projection definition, primarily used by advanced software to correctly orient complex sky projections.

LATPOLE.content: This keyword specifies the native latitude of the celestial pole in degrees, relevant for certain specialized World Coordinate System (WCS) projections. It's a technical parameter defining part of how sky coordinates are mapped onto the image pixels.
LATPOLE.relevance_comment: This is a low-level WCS parameter that is not directly consumed by most end-users, who typically work with the transformed RA/Dec coordinates.

S_REGION.content: This keyword describes the precise spatial footprint of the observation on the sky using the IVOA's STC-S (Space-Time Coordinate System) format. It provides a polygon or region that delineates the exact area covered by the data, useful for database queries and visualization.
S_REGION.relevance_comment: This is highly relevant for understanding the exact sky coverage of an observation, valuable for search, display, and data comparison.

A_ORDER.content: This keyword specifies the degree of the forward 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used for distortion correction along the first image axis (x-axis). SIP coefficients are used to mathematically model and correct for optical distortions in the instrument.
A_ORDER.relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model, not directly useful for general users who benefit from the corrected image but don't need the polynomial specifics.

B_ORDER.content: This keyword specifies the degree of the forward 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used for distortion correction along the second image axis (y-axis). SIP coefficients are used to mathematically model and correct for optical distortions in the instrument.
B_ORDER.relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model, not directly useful for general users who benefit from the corrected image but don't need the polynomial specifics.

AP_ORDER.content: This keyword specifies the degree of the inverse 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used to reverse distortion corrections along the first image axis. These polynomials allow mapping from distorted image coordinates back to ideal pixel coordinates.
AP_ORDER.relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model for inverse transformations, not directly useful for general users.

BP_ORDER.content: This keyword specifies the degree of the inverse 'Simple Imaging Polynomial' (SIP) used to reverse distortion corrections along the second image axis. These polynomials allow mapping from distorted image coordinates back to ideal pixel coordinates.
BP_ORDER.relevance_comment: This is a technical detail of the WCS distortion model for inverse transformations, not directly useful for general users.

SUBARRAY.content: This keyword indicates if the observation captured the full detector frame or a specific, smaller region (subarray). It often provides a descriptive name for the subarray used, such as 'FULL' for full frame or a custom name.
SUBARRAY.relevance_comment: This is useful for understanding whether the image represents the full detector field of view or a specific partial readout, impacting interpretation and visualization.

SUBSTRT1.content: This keyword indicates the starting pixel column (axis 1) of the observed subarray within the full detector frame. It specifies the leftmost pixel of the subarray in the original detector coordinates.
SUBSTRT1.relevance_comment: This provides granular information about the subarray's position, useful for advanced visualization, comparison, or understanding detector layout.

SUBSTRT2.content: This keyword indicates the starting pixel row (axis 2) of the observed subarray within the full detector frame. It specifies the bottommost pixel of the subarray in the original detector coordinates.
SUBSTRT2.relevance_comment: This provides granular information about the subarray's position, useful for advanced visualization, comparison, or understanding detector layout.

SUBSIZE1.content: This keyword indicates the number of pixels along the first axis (width) of the observed subarray. It defines the size of the captured region in the x-direction.
SUBSIZE1.relevance_comment: This provides granular information about the subarray's dimensions, useful for advanced visualization, comparison, or understanding detector layout.

SUBSIZE2.content: This keyword specifies the dimension of the detector subarray along its second axis, indicating how many pixels were read out in that direction. It's crucial for understanding the size and extent of the captured image data when only a portion of the full detector was used.
SUBSIZE2.relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the physical dimensions of a subarray image, relevant for advanced users analyzing instrument modes.

FASTAXIS.content: This keyword describes which detector axis (1 or 2) corresponds to the faster readout direction, and whether it's read out in the positive or negative direction. This information is critical for instrument scientists or advanced users performing detailed noise analysis or understanding detector effects.
FASTAXIS.relevance_comment: Primarily relevant for instrument specialists or those performing detailed detector analysis, not typical end-users.

SLOWAXIS.content: This keyword indicates which detector axis (1 or 2) corresponds to the slower readout direction, including the positive or negative readout direction. Like FASTAXIS, it's essential for understanding how the detector was operated and for analyzing specific detector artifacts or noise patterns.
SLOWAXIS.relevance_comment: Primarily relevant for instrument specialists or those performing detailed detector analysis, not typical end-users.

APERNAME.content: This keyword provides the official name of the science aperture used for the observation, as defined by the Spacecraft & Observatory Control (S&OC) system. It tells users exactly which instrument configuration and field of view was selected for the observation, linking the data to specific instrument modes.
APERNAME.relevance_comment: Essential for understanding the specific instrument configuration and field of view used for the observation.

PPS_APER.content: This keyword stores the initial aperture name as it was specified in the Planning and Scheduling System (PPS) before any internal processing or translation. It can be useful for tracking the origin of an observation's configuration within the mission's planning pipeline.
PPS_APER.relevance_comment: Provides context on the aperture name's origin within the planning system, primarily useful for pipeline traceability or specific debugging.

NUMDTHPT.content: This keyword specifies the total count of individual positions included in the complete dither pattern for the observation. It helps users understand the overall strategy employed to cover the target area or mitigate detector artifacts through multiple shifted exposures.
NUMDTHPT.relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the overall observation strategy and the full extent of a dithered observation.

PATT_NUM.content: This keyword indicates the sequential number of the current observation within its complete dither pattern. It allows users to identify where this specific exposure fits into the larger dither sequence, which is crucial for combining multiple dithered images.
PATT_NUM.relevance_comment: Crucial for identifying the specific position of an exposure within a dither sequence, vital for data combination.

XOFFSET.content: This keyword provides the offset in arcseconds along the X-axis from the nominal starting point of the dither pattern to the actual observation's position. It's essential for precisely aligning and combining individual dithered images to create a mosaic or improve spatial sampling.
XOFFSET.relevance_comment: Essential for accurately aligning and combining individual dithered exposures for improved data quality.

YOFFSET.content: This keyword provides the offset in arcseconds along the Y-axis from the nominal starting point of the dither pattern to the current observation's position. Similar to XOFFSET, it's vital for precisely aligning and combining multiple dithered exposures to achieve the intended scientific outcome.
YOFFSET.relevance_comment: Essential for accurately aligning and combining individual dithered exposures for improved data quality.

PATTTYPE.content: This keyword describes the specific strategy or design of the dither pattern employed for the observation. Knowing the pattern type helps users understand the scientific goals behind the dithering (e.g., full coverage, artifact mitigation) and guide their data processing choices.
PATTTYPE.relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the high-level strategy behind the dither pattern, which informs data analysis and interpretation.

PATTSIZE.content: This keyword indicates the overall size or extent of the dither pattern used during data acquisition. Dithering is a technique where the telescope is slightly moved between exposures to improve image quality, sample detector pixels better, and remove cosmic rays or detector defects. The size refers to the spatial extent of these shifts.
PATTSIZE.relevance_comment: Understanding the dither pattern size is useful for advanced users evaluating data quality and spatial resolution.

PATTSTRT.content: This specifies which step or position in a multi-point dither sequence was the first one executed for the current observation. When a dither pattern has many points, observations might start at an arbitrary point in the sequence, especially if it's a continuation of a larger program.
PATTSTRT.relevance_comment: This keyword is primarily an operational detail for pipeline processing and instrument teams, with little direct end-user relevance.

PATTNPTS.content: This keyword indicates the total number of distinct positions the telescope visited as part of a cycling dither pattern. A higher number of points generally leads to better sampling of the detector and more robust cosmic ray rejection when the individual dithered images are combined.
PATTNPTS.relevance_comment: Knowing the number of dither points helps users understand the completeness and quality potential of the combined data, particularly for artifact rejection.

NUMDSETS.content: This specifies how many complete cycles of a smaller, typically 4-point, dither pattern were executed. Some dither strategies involve repeating a foundational pattern multiple times to achieve specific noise reduction or image quality goals.
NUMDSETS.relevance_comment: This provides insight into the observation's redundancy and helps assess the potential for noise reduction or coverage for specific dither strategies.

DSETSTRT.content: This keyword indicates which specific set number of a repeated dither pattern was the first one observed within a larger sequence. It tracks the starting point for multi-set dither strategies, similar to tracking the starting point within a single dither pattern.
DSETSTRT.relevance_comment: This is an internal detail for data pipeline and instrument teams, holding little direct value for typical end-user analysis.

DITHDIRC.content: This indicates the general direction of the dither steps, often referring to whether the movements are sequentially positive or negative in a given coordinate axis. This can be relevant for understanding the precise sequence of small movements performed by the telescope.
DITHDIRC.relevance_comment: This is a highly specific operational detail of dither execution that rarely impacts the final scientific interpretation for most end-users.

DITHOPFR.content: This keyword describes whether the dither pattern was specifically designed to optimize observations of point sources (like stars) or extended sources (like galaxies). Different optimization types use dither step sizes and patterns to best sample the target morphology and mitigate detector effects.
DITHOPFR.relevance_comment: This is very useful as it directly tells the user if the dither strategy was appropriate for the observed target type, influencing photometry or surface brightness measurements.

DITHPNTS.content: This keyword provides a list or description of the specific positions visited during a sparse cycling dither pattern. Sparse dithers typically involve larger steps designed to move a target across different detector regions to mitigate artifacts or improve flat-fielding.
DITHPNTS.relevance_comment: For advanced users, knowing the exact dither positions can be crucial for understanding detector coverage, artifact mitigation, and for re-processing data.

MRSPRCHN.content: This keyword specifies which of the MIRI Medium Resolution Spectrometer (MRS) channels the dither pattern was specifically optimized for. The MRS has multiple channels covering different wavelength ranges with varying pixel scales, requiring channel-specific dither strategies.
MRSPRCHN.relevance_comment: This is important for MIRI MRS users to confirm the dither strategy was appropriate for the analyzed channel, impacting spectral extraction and data quality.

NOD_TYPE.content: For NIRSpec Multi-Object Spectrometer (MOS) observations, this keyword describes the type of 'nodding' pattern used. Nodding is a technique where the telescope moves the target between different micro-shutter array (MSA) shutters to allow for accurate background subtraction and improved data quality, especially for faint targets.
NOD_TYPE.relevance_comment: This is crucial for NIRSpec MOS users to understand how background subtraction was handled, which directly impacts the quality of extracted spectra.

PRIDTYPE.content: This keyword indicates the specific type of primary dither pattern used during the observation, along with the total number of points in that pattern. Dithering involves slightly shifting the telescope's pointing between exposures to improve image quality, fill in detector gaps, and mitigate cosmic ray effects.
PRIDTYPE.relevance_comment: This keyword is useful for advanced users to understand the observation strategy and assess data quality or completeness.

PRIDTPTS.content: This keyword specifies the total number of individual pointing positions in the primary dither pattern performed by the Fine Guidance Sensor (FGS) during the observation. A higher number of dither points generally allows for better sampling and artifact rejection.
PRIDTPTS.relevance_comment: Understanding the number of dither points helps advanced users assess the quality and coverage of combined data products.

SUBPXPAT.content: This keyword describes the type of subpixel dither pattern applied, which is designed to sample the sky at resolutions finer than a single detector pixel. Common types include 'STANDARD' or 'SMALL-GRID-DITHER'. Using a subpixel dither improves the effective resolution of the final image.
SUBPXPAT.relevance_comment: This is valuable for users interested in high-resolution imaging, as it directly impacts the achievable detail in the final processed data.

SUBPXPTS.content: This keyword indicates the number of distinct pointing positions in the subpixel dither pattern. More points in a subpixel dither pattern contribute to a more finely sampled final image, which is crucial for achieving optimal spatial resolution.
SUBPXPTS.relevance_comment: Knowing the number of subpixel dither points is useful for advanced users evaluating the potential resolution and quality of the final image.

SMGRDPAT.content: This keyword specifies the particular name or configuration of the NIRCam small grid dither pattern used. Small grid dithers are a specialized type of dither often employed for specific science cases requiring precise subpixel sampling or mosaic patterns.
SMGRDPAT.relevance_comment: This keyword is relevant for specialized NIRCam users who need to know the exact dither strategy for their specific analysis.

NRIMDTPT.content: This keyword indicates the number of dither points specifically used for direct imaging observations with the NIRISS instrument. This value informs how many distinct positions the instrument observed as part of its dither sequence.
NRIMDTPT.relevance_comment: This is useful for NIRISS instrument specialists or advanced users analyzing direct imaging data to understand data coverage.

SPATNSTP.content: This keyword specifies the total number of steps or positions taken in the spatial direction during a MIRI Low-Resolution Spectroscopy (LRS) observation. These steps are part of a dither pattern to improve spatial sampling or remove detector artifacts.
SPATNSTP.relevance_comment: This keyword is valuable for users working with MIRI LRS data to understand the spatial sampling of their spectra.

SPATSTEP.content: This keyword provides the physical distance, in arcseconds, between successive steps in the spatial direction for a MIRI LRS observation. This value quantifies the actual offset applied during spatial dithering.
SPATSTEP.relevance_comment: This precise measurement is highly useful for advanced users performing detailed spatial analysis or source extraction from MIRI LRS data.

SPAT_NUM.content: This keyword indicates the specific position number of the current exposure within the larger sequence of spatial steps for a MIRI LRS observation. It helps to identify where this particular frame fits into the overall dither pattern.
SPAT_NUM.relevance_comment: This keyword is important for advanced users needing to track and combine individual frames from a dither sequence.

SPECNSTP.content: This keyword specifies the total number of steps or positions taken in the spectral direction during a MIRI Low-Resolution Spectroscopy (LRS) observation. These steps are part of a dither pattern designed to mitigate fringing and improve spectral fidelity.
SPECNSTP.relevance_comment: This keyword is valuable for users working with MIRI LRS data to understand the spectral sampling and artifact mitigation strategy.

SPECSTEP.content: This keyword indicates the physical distance in arcseconds between successive exposures when dithered along the spectral direction for MIRI LRS observations. It's important for understanding how the instrument sampled the spectrum to mitigate detector artifacts or improve resolution.
SPECSTEP.relevance_comment: This details a specific dither parameter, useful for understanding data quality and processing, but not for casual viewing.

SPEC_NUM.content: This identifies the specific position within a sequence of spectral dither steps for MIRI LRS observations. It helps researchers track which part of the dither pattern a particular exposure corresponds to, crucial for co-adding and artifact removal.
SPEC_NUM.relevance_comment: This indicates the dither position, useful for advanced data processing and quality control.

SPTOFFST.content: This keyword specifies the spatial offset in arcseconds from the starting point of the dither pattern for MIRI LRS observations. It helps determine the precise on-sky position of the observation relative to the overall pattern.
SPTOFFST.relevance_comment: This describes a spatial offset within a dither pattern, relevant for precise astrometry and advanced data reduction.

SPCOFFST.content: This keyword specifies the spectral offset in arcseconds from the starting point of the dither pattern for MIRI LRS observations. It helps determine how the instrument's detector elements were moved along the spectral direction relative to the overall pattern.
SPCOFFST.relevance_comment: This describes a spectral offset within a dither pattern, relevant for precise wavelength calibration and advanced data reduction.

VISITYPE.content: This keyword describes the overall type of observation visit, such as whether it's a primary target, a calibration, or an engineering test. It helps users understand the primary scientific or operational goal of the observation.
VISITYPE.relevance_comment: This offers important high-level context about the observation's purpose, useful for understanding the data.

VSTSTART.content: This keyword records the precise UTC date and time when the entire observation visit began. It's crucial for time-sensitive analyses, linking observations to external events, and understanding the overall timeline of data acquisition.
VSTSTART.relevance_comment: The start time of an entire visit is highly relevant for understanding when the observation took place.

VISITSTA.content: This keyword indicates the overall outcome or status of the observation visit, such as 'SUCCESSFUL,' 'DATALOSS,' or 'FAILED.' It's critical for users to quickly assess the quality and usability of the data.
VISITSTA.relevance_comment: This provides critical information about the success and quality of the observation, highly relevant for data assessment.

NEXPOSUR.content: This keyword specifies the total number of individual exposures that were planned to be taken during this entire observation visit. It provides context for the complete dataset and helps verify if all expected data frames were acquired.
NEXPOSUR.relevance_comment: This provides useful context about the scope of the observation and data completeness.

INTARGET.content: This boolean keyword indicates whether at least one exposure within the observation visit was designated as an 'internal target,' typically meaning it's pointed at an internal calibration source rather than an external astronomical object. It helps differentiate between science and calibration data.
INTARGET.relevance_comment: This helps users understand if the data pertains to an astronomical target or an internal calibration.

TARGOOPP.content: This boolean keyword indicates whether the observation visit was scheduled as a 'Target of Opportunity' (ToO), meaning it was triggered by a transient event like a supernova or gamma-ray burst. It provides critical context about the transient nature of the observed phenomenon.
TARGOOPP.relevance_comment: This provides essential context about the nature of the observation, indicating if it was triggered by a transient event.

TSOVISIT.content: This boolean keyword indicates if the observation was part of a Time Series Observation (TSO) program. TSOs are designed to capture changes over time, often for exoplanet transits or stellar variability studies, making this crucial for understanding the data's scientific purpose and appropriate analysis techniques.
TSOVISIT.relevance_comment: It informs users about a specific observing mode that significantly impacts scientific interpretation and data analysis strategies.

EXP_ONLY.content: This keyword indicates if the exposure was taken using special commanding, bypassing the standard Science Instrument (SI) configuration process. This typically signifies an engineering or diagnostic exposure, rather than a scientific observation intended for public release and analysis.
EXP_ONLY.relevance_comment: This keyword primarily identifies non-standard or engineering exposures, which are rarely relevant for typical end-user scientific analysis.

CROWDFLD.content: This boolean keyword indicates whether the Fine Guidance Sensors (FGSes) encountered a crowded star field during the observation. Operating in a crowded field can potentially affect the accuracy and stability of guide star acquisition and tracking, which might subtly influence the overall pointing stability and astrometric quality of the data.
CROWDFLD.relevance_comment: This offers insight into potential pointing stability challenges, which can be useful for advanced users assessing data quality.

GS_ORDER.content: This integer specifies the index or sequential order of the particular guide star within a list of selected guide stars for an observation. It is an internal identifier used to distinguish between multiple guide stars that might have been considered or used.
GS_ORDER.relevance_comment: This is an internal identifier for guide star selection, not generally relevant for typical scientific data analysis by end-users.

GSSTRTTM.content: This keyword records the Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) when the guide star acquisition or tracking activity commenced for the observation. It provides a precise timestamp for when the telescope began actively using the guide star for fine pointing.
GSSTRTTM.relevance_comment: Provides a precise timestamp for the start of guiding activity, which can be useful for detailed timing analysis or correlating with other events.

GSENDTIM.content: This keyword records the Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) when the guide star acquisition or tracking activity concluded. Paired with GSSTRTTM, it defines the full duration during which the guide star was actively used to stabilize the telescope's pointing.
GSENDTIM.relevance_comment: Provides a precise timestamp for the end of guiding activity, useful for detailed timing analysis or assessing guiding duration.

GDSTARID.content: This keyword provides the unique 10-character, base-36 identifier for the specific guide star used from the Guide Star Catalog 2 (GSC2). This ID allows researchers to look up detailed properties of the guide star in star catalogs.
GDSTARID.relevance_comment: This identifies the specific guide star used, allowing advanced users to look up its properties for detailed analysis or verification.

GS_RA.content: This keyword specifies the ICRS (International Celestial Reference System) Right Ascension coordinate of the guide star, measured in degrees. It is a fundamental celestial coordinate defining the guide star's position on the sky, crucial for understanding the telescope's pointing context.
GS_RA.relevance_comment: Provides the precise celestial Right Ascension of the guide star, which is crucial for understanding pointing context and verifying astrometry.

GS_DEC.content: This keyword specifies the ICRS (International Celestial Reference System) Declination coordinate of the guide star, measured in degrees. Along with GS_RA, it precisely defines the guide star's position on the sky and is essential for astrometric analyses.
GS_DEC.relevance_comment: Provides the precise celestial Declination of the guide star, crucial for understanding pointing context and verifying astrometry.

GS_URA.content: This keyword provides the uncertainty in the Right Ascension (RA) measurement of the guide star, expressed in arcseconds. This value indicates the precision of the guide star's known position, which directly impacts the overall astrometric accuracy and pointing stability of the observation.
GS_URA.relevance_comment: This provides a crucial metric for advanced users to assess the precision of the guide star's Right Ascension and its potential impact on pointing accuracy.

GS_UDEC.content: This keyword indicates the uncertainty in the celestial declination of the guide star used to keep the telescope pointed accurately. It tells astronomers how precisely the guide star's vertical position is known, which impacts the overall pointing stability and accuracy of the observation.
GS_UDEC.relevance_comment: This guide star uncertainty is mainly relevant for advanced users diagnosing pointing issues, not for general data consumption.

GS_MAG.content: This keyword specifies the magnitude (brightness) of the guide star as measured by the Fine Guidance Sensor (FGS) detector. The guide star's brightness is crucial for assessing the reliability of the pointing system and for understanding the signal-to-noise ratio of the guiding process.
GS_MAG.relevance_comment: Knowing the guide star's brightness helps users assess observation quality and potential pointing stability.

GS_UMAG.content: This keyword represents the uncertainty in the measured magnitude of the guide star. It quantifies how precisely the guide star's brightness is known, which can be relevant for understanding the reliability of the guiding process and any potential issues.
GS_UMAG.relevance_comment: This guide star magnitude uncertainty is mainly relevant for advanced users diagnosing pointing issues, not for general data consumption.

GS_V3_PA.content: This keyword gives the position angle of the guide star with respect to the V3 axis of the telescope, measured in degrees. It helps describe the precise orientation of the guide star relative to the instrument's field of view, which can be important for understanding roll stability and field rotation.
GS_V3_PA.relevance_comment: This keyword provides specific technical details about guide star orientation, useful for advanced pointing analysis but less for general users.

PCS_MODE.content: This keyword describes the mode in which the Pointing Control System (PCS) was operating during the observation. Examples include 'FINEGUIDE' for precise pointing using a guide star, or 'TRACK' for tracking a moving target. This information is vital for understanding the pointing stability and quality of the observation.
PCS_MODE.relevance_comment: Knowing the pointing mode is crucial for understanding the stability and quality of the observation.

VISITEND.content: This keyword records the Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) when a specific astronomical visit, or observation block, concluded. It helps track the overall timeline of observations and can be used for scheduling or correlating with other events.
VISITEND.relevance_comment: The end time of an observation visit is important for understanding the overall observation timeline and context.

BARTDELT.content: This keyword provides the calculated time difference (in seconds) that must be added to the UTC timestamp to correct it to the barycentric frame of reference. This correction accounts for the Earth's motion around the Sun, ensuring accurate timing for phenomena like exoplanet transits or pulsars.
BARTDELT.relevance_comment: This barycentric time correction is essential for precise timing analysis, making it highly relevant for researchers in time-domain astronomy.

BSTRTIME.content: This keyword gives the starting time of the observation exposure, corrected to the barycenter of the Solar System and expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). Using barycentric time ensures that timing measurements are consistent regardless of the Earth's position.
BSTRTIME.relevance_comment: The barycentric-corrected start time is critical for precise time-domain analysis, making it very important for advanced users and researchers.

BENDTIME.content: This keyword records the ending time of the observation exposure, corrected to the barycenter of the Solar System and expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). This provides a precise, astronomically relevant timestamp for the conclusion of data collection.
BENDTIME.relevance_comment: The barycentric-corrected end time is critical for precise time-domain analysis, making it very important for advanced users and researchers.

BMIDTIME.content: This keyword specifies the mid-point time of the observation exposure, corrected to the barycenter of the Solar System and expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). The mid-exposure time is often the most representative timestamp for an observation, especially when observing variable phenomena.
BMIDTIME.relevance_comment: The barycentric-corrected mid-exposure time is crucial for precise time-domain analysis and often the most representative timestamp for an observation.

HELIDELT.content: This keyword indicates the time difference, in seconds, between the observation time in UTC and the equivalent time if the observation were made from the Sun's center (heliocentric). This correction accounts for the telescope's motion around the Sun, which is vital for high-precision timing studies of variable sources.
HELIDELT.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced timing analyses requiring heliocentric correction, but less critical for general data viewing or basic analysis.

HSTRTIME.content: This keyword specifies the starting time of the observation in the heliocentric reference frame, expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). It provides the precise beginning of data acquisition as if observed from the Sun's center, which is important for accurate timing studies across different datasets and observatories.
HSTRTIME.relevance_comment: Important for precise timing analysis, offering a standardized start time in a heliocentric frame.

HENDTIME.content: This keyword indicates the ending time of the observation in the heliocentric reference frame, expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). It marks the precise conclusion of data acquisition as if observed from the Sun's center, complementing HSTRTIME for accurate duration and timing analyses.
HENDTIME.relevance_comment: Important for precise timing analysis, offering a standardized end time in a heliocentric frame.

HMIDTIME.content: This keyword gives the midpoint time of the observation in the heliocentric reference frame, expressed as a Modified Julian Date (MJD). It represents the effective central time of the exposure as if observed from the Sun's center, often used as the primary timestamp for scientific measurements that assume a single observation time.
HMIDTIME.relevance_comment: Highly useful for associating scientific measurements with a single, precise, and standardized observation time.

ASNPOOL.content: This keyword stores the name of the association pool file used as input for the data processing pipeline. An association pool defines how related raw data (like multiple exposures of the same target) are grouped for combined processing into higher-level scientific products.
ASNPOOL.relevance_comment: Primarily relevant for understanding pipeline provenance and debugging, rather than direct scientific interpretation by end-users.

ASNTABLE.content: This keyword provides the name of the association table from which the current data product was created by the pipeline. The association table details the specific rules and groupings used to combine raw exposures into the final scientific product.
ASNTABLE.relevance_comment: Useful for tracking the data's pipeline processing and how individual files were grouped into a product.

BKGLEVEL.content: This keyword specifies the overall background signal level, typically in data units per pixel, computed during the 'skymatch' pipeline step. This value represents the general diffuse emission or instrumental noise present across the image or spectrum.
BKGLEVEL.relevance_comment: Provides crucial information about the estimated background noise, which is important for evaluating data quality and signal-to-noise.

BKGSUB.content: This boolean keyword indicates whether the background signal, as identified by BKGLEVEL, has been subtracted from the data ('T' for True, 'F' for False). It is essential for understanding the processing state and interpreting the pixel values of the image or spectrum.
BKGSUB.relevance_comment: Essential for knowing if the data has been debiased by background subtraction, fundamentally impacting data interpretation.

MASTERBG.content: This keyword stores the name of the 1-D master background spectrum file utilized by the pipeline during processing. Master background files are pre-calibrated reference files used to remove diffuse emission or instrumental noise from scientific observations.
MASTERBG.relevance_comment: Primarily useful for advanced users or pipeline developers who need to understand the exact reference files used for background subtraction.

FILENAME.content: This keyword simply stores the name of the FITS file itself. While seemingly redundant, it can be useful for internal tracking within data pipelines or for verifying data integrity if files are renamed or moved.
FILENAME.relevance_comment: While fundamental, the filename is usually known to the user who opened the file, making its header presence redundant for most end-user applications.

FILETYPE.content: This keyword indicates the general category or purpose of the data stored in the FITS file, such as 'SCIENCE' for observation data or 'CALIBRATION' for reference files. Knowing the file type helps users quickly understand what kind of data they are looking at and its intended use.
FILETYPE.relevance_comment: This is highly relevant as it immediately informs the user about the fundamental nature of the file's content.

ROOTNAME.content: The root name serves as a unique identifier for a specific observation or dataset, often linking together multiple associated files (e.g., raw data, calibrated images, and derived products). It is crucial for organizing and tracking all components belonging to a single astronomical observation program.
ROOTNAME.relevance_comment: This keyword is very useful for users to group and manage related files from a common observation set.

IMAGETYP.content: This keyword specifies the type of astronomical exposure, such as 'OBJECT' for a science observation, 'BIAS' for a zero-exposure frame, 'DARK' for a long-exposure with no light, or 'FLAT' for a uniform illumination frame. It is fundamental for understanding the purpose of an image and its role in the data calibration process.
IMAGETYP.relevance_comment: This is an essential keyword for all users, as it dictates how an image should be interpreted and whether it's science data or a calibration aid.

LINENUM.content: This keyword refers to an administrative line number from the original telescope proposal's log sheet. It is primarily used internally by observatories for tracking observations during scheduling and execution.
LINENUM.relevance_comment: This is a purely administrative identifier with very little direct relevance for most end-users of an astronomy app.

POSTARG1.content: POSTARG1 indicates an offset applied to the telescope's pointing position along the first spatial axis, typically specified in arcseconds or pixels. These offsets are often used for techniques like dithering, which involves taking multiple images at slightly different positions to fill in detector gaps or improve image quality.
POSTARG1.relevance_comment: This keyword is useful for advanced users analyzing dither patterns or requiring precise pointing information, but less critical for casual viewers.

POSTARG2.content: POSTARG2 indicates an offset applied to the telescope's pointing position along the second spatial axis, similar to POSTARG1. These offsets are crucial for understanding the exact pointing of an observation relative to a nominal target position and for combining dithered exposures.
POSTARG2.relevance_comment: Similar to POSTARG1, this keyword is useful for advanced users involved in astrometry or dither analysis.

CAL_VER.content: This keyword records the specific version number of the software pipeline used to process and calibrate the astronomical data. It is important for reproducibility and understanding any potential changes or improvements in data reduction algorithms.
CAL_VER.relevance_comment: While not essential for every user, researchers and advanced users find this useful for understanding data provenance and comparing results.

CAL_VCS.content: This keyword provides an even more precise identifier for the calibration software, often a git hash or tag from the version control system. It allows for exact reproduction of the software build used for processing.
CAL_VCS.relevance_comment: This is primarily relevant for pipeline developers or users needing extremely precise software versioning for debugging or exact reproduction, not for general users.

CRDS_VER.content: This keyword specifies the version of the CRDS (Calibration Reference Data System) client software used to automatically select the appropriate calibration reference files. CRDS ensures that the correct darks, flats, and other reference data are applied during processing.
CRDS_VER.relevance_comment: This information is largely internal to the data processing pipeline and holds minimal direct value for the typical end-user viewing the calibrated data.

CRDS_CTX.content: This keyword identifies the specific 'context' or mapping file (often a PMAP file) within the CRDS system that governed the selection of calibration reference files for the observation. It dictates which versions of reference files were considered valid for the given data.
CRDS_CTX.relevance_comment: This keyword describes an internal aspect of the calibration system's configuration and has little direct relevance to a typical end-user.

SDP_VER.content: This keyword indicates the specific version of the scientific data processing pipeline software used to generate this data product. It is important for ensuring the reproducibility of results and understanding the exact algorithms and calibrations applied to the raw observations.
SDP_VER.relevance_comment: This keyword is primarily useful for advanced users to track data processing provenance and reproducibility.

PRD_VER.content: This keyword specifies the version of the Project Reference Database that contains crucial calibration files, instrument models, and other reference data utilized during the processing of this observation. It's vital for understanding the exact calibration context applied to the scientific data.
PRD_VER.relevance_comment: This keyword provides context for the specific reference data used in calibration, primarily relevant for advanced users and pipeline developers.

OSS_VER.content: This keyword records the version number of the Observatory Scheduling Software used to plan and schedule the astronomical observation. While important for observatory operations, it typically does not directly influence the scientific interpretation of the resulting data for an end-user.
OSS_VER.relevance_comment: This is an internal operational detail with minimal direct relevance for astronomical data analysis by end-users.

VA_SCALE.content: This represents the velocity aberration scale factor, a correction applied to account for the apparent shift in object positions due to the observatory's motion relative to the solar system barycenter. It's a critical detail for achieving high-precision astrometric accuracy in the data.
VA_SCALE.relevance_comment: This factor is an internal calibration parameter important for high-precision astrometry, primarily relevant for advanced users working on specific analyses.

PHOTMJSR.content: This is a photometric conversion factor indicating the flux density, in MegaJanskys per steradian, that produces one count per second in the detector. It is an essential value for converting the raw observed counts into physically meaningful flux units for quantitative scientific analysis.
PHOTMJSR.relevance_comment: This keyword is crucial for converting observed counts into physical flux density units, making it highly relevant for quantitative photometric analysis.

PIXAR_SR.content: This keyword specifies the nominal angular area of a single pixel on the sky, expressed in steradians. It is useful for converting flux density per steradian to flux density per pixel, and for calculations involving surface brightness measurements.
PIXAR_SR.relevance_comment: This keyword provides the nominal pixel area, essential for photometric calculations involving surface brightness or converting flux units.

PIXAR_A2.content: This keyword defines the nominal angular area of a single pixel on the sky, expressed in square arcseconds. It is a fundamental parameter for understanding the spatial resolution of the image and for performing photometric conversions involving the image scale.
PIXAR_A2.relevance_comment: This keyword is highly relevant as it provides the fundamental pixel scale in a widely understood astronomical unit, crucial for understanding image resolution and performing photometric conversions.

HGA_MOVE.content: This boolean flag indicates whether the High Gain Antenna (HGA) of the observatory was repositioned at any point while the scientific data was being collected. An HGA move can sometimes introduce minor disturbances, which may be relevant for very sensitive observations.
HGA_MOVE.relevance_comment: This boolean flag is useful for advanced users to diagnose potential data quality issues, indicating if an observatory operation occurred during exposure.

PWFSEET.content: This timestamp, given in Modified Julian Date (MJD), records the exact end time of the wavefront sensing (WFS) exposure that immediately preceded the current scientific observation. It provides context for the timing of instrument calibrations relative to the science data.
PWFSEET.relevance_comment: This is an internal timing detail related to wavefront sensing, primarily relevant for instrument engineers and advanced debugging.

NWFSEST.content: This timestamp, provided in Modified Julian Date (MJD), indicates the exact start time of the next scheduled wavefront sensing (WFS) exposure following the current scientific observation. It is an internal operational detail for tracking instrument calibration cycles.
NWFSEST.relevance_comment: This is an internal timing detail related to wavefront sensing, primarily relevant for instrument engineers and advanced debugging.

ERRTYPE.content: This keyword specifies what kind of uncertainty data is stored in a corresponding error array, such as direct errors, inverse errors, variance, or inverse variance. Understanding the error type is crucial for correctly interpreting the statistical reliability of the astronomical measurements. Knowing this helps users apply the correct statistical methods for analysis.
ERRTYPE.relevance_comment: This keyword is important for correctly understanding and utilizing the uncertainty information associated with the astronomical data.

FA1VALUE.content: This keyword indicates the desired position for the first of several focus actuators, which are mechanical components used to precisely adjust the telescope's focus. It's an internal engineering setting reflecting the requested optical configuration. This value is part of the fine-tuning of the instrument's optics.
FA1VALUE.relevance_comment: This is a low-level engineering parameter that provides minimal direct value to most end-users interested in the astronomical data.

FA2VALUE.content: Similar to FA1VALUE, this keyword specifies the requested absolute position for the second focus actuator. These actuators work together to achieve the desired optical focus for the instrument. It reflects a specific internal setup of the instrument.
FA2VALUE.relevance_comment: This is a low-level engineering parameter that provides minimal direct value to most end-users interested in the astronomical data.

FA3VALUE.content: This keyword records the requested absolute position for the third focus actuator. Together with the other actuators, it helps finely tune the instrument's optical focus. It's an internal instrument setting.
FA3VALUE.relevance_comment: This is a low-level engineering parameter that provides minimal direct value to most end-users interested in the astronomical data.

FCSRLPOS.content: This keyword indicates the relative position of the NIRSpec Refocus Mechanism Assembly (RMA) in motor steps, reflecting its setting for optical focus adjustments. It describes the precise physical configuration of an internal instrument component at the time of observation.
FCSRLPOS.relevance_comment: This is a detailed instrument engineering parameter that offers little direct utility to most end-users analyzing the scientific data.

RMA_POS.content: This keyword stores the actual measured position of the NIRSpec Refocus Mechanism Assembly (RMA) as reported by its hall-position sensor. It provides a real-time reading of a critical component for optical focus, indicating its true position during observation.
RMA_POS.relevance_comment: This is a detailed instrument engineering parameter that offers little direct utility to most end-users analyzing the scientific data.

ROIS.content: This keyword defines the spatial radius of a 'region of interest' used during data processing, typically for integral field unit (IFU) observations. It indicates the angular extent around a target that was considered relevant for extraction or analysis. Understanding this helps advanced users assess the spatial context of extracted spectra.
ROIS.relevance_comment: This keyword can be useful for advanced users to understand the spatial extent of processed data in IFU observations.

ROIW.content: This keyword specifies the radius of a 'region of interest' in the wavelength dimension, again typically for IFU data processing. It describes the spectral extent considered important for analysis or extraction. This helps advanced users understand the spectral coverage of processed data.
ROIW.relevance_comment: This keyword can be useful for advanced users to understand the spectral extent of processed data in IFU observations.

WPOWER.content: This keyword indicates the exponent used in a distance-weighting scheme when combining data from individual spatial pixels (spaxels) in an Integral Field Unit (IFU) observation. It controls how strongly the flux from nearby spaxels contributes to a combined measurement. This is a processing parameter.
WPOWER.relevance_comment: This parameter is relevant for advanced users who need to understand the details of how IFU spaxel data was weighted and combined during processing.

WTYPE.content: This keyword describes the specific method or algorithm used for weighting and combining fluxes from multiple spaxels in an Integral Field Unit (IFU) observation. It provides information on how different spaxels contributed to the final flux measurements, such as 'uniform' or 'distance-weighted'.
WTYPE.relevance_comment: This keyword is important for advanced users seeking to understand the specific weighting algorithm applied during the combination of IFU spaxel data.

SCATFILE.content: This keyword stores the name of an intermediate file containing a catalog of sources detected in the image, generated during the data processing pipeline. It's used internally by the pipeline to track processing steps, especially related to source detection and astrometric solutions.
SCATFILE.relevance_comment: This filename refers to an internal pipeline product that most users will not directly need or understand.

TCATFILE.content: This keyword specifies the filename of the source catalog used by the 'tweakreg' pipeline step, which performs precise astrometric alignment of images. It helps the pipeline keep track of which catalog was used for spatial registration adjustments.
TCATFILE.relevance_comment: This filename refers to an internal pipeline product used for astrometric alignment that most users will not directly need.

COMMENT.content: This keyword provides free-form textual comments from data producers or users. It can contain any human-readable information that might be useful, such as notes on observing conditions, processing steps, or data quality.
COMMENT.relevance_comment: Provides human-readable context or additional notes about the data which is often valuable to end-users.

HISTORY.content: This keyword records a historical log of processing steps applied to the data, often including timestamps, software versions, and parameters used. It's crucial for understanding the provenance and transformations the data has undergone.
HISTORY.relevance_comment: Provides a critical log of data processing steps, essential for understanding data provenance and quality.

PHOTFLAM.content: This keyword represents the inverse sensitivity of the instrument in a specific filter, indicating the flux density of a source (in erg/s/cm²/Å) that would produce one count per second. It's essential for converting raw data counts into absolute flux units, especially for HST data.
PHOTFLAM.relevance_comment: Crucial for converting raw data counts into physical flux units, which is a fundamental photometric task.

PHOTZPT.content: This keyword specifies the magnitude zero point for the ST magnitude system, which is used to convert instrumental magnitudes to standard calibrated magnitudes. It's a key value for photometric analysis, particularly with HST data.
PHOTZPT.relevance_comment: Essential for converting instrumental magnitudes into a standard magnitude system.

PHOTPLAM.content: This keyword defines the 'pivot wavelength' of the filter, which is a single characteristic wavelength representing the effective center of the filter's bandpass. It's crucial for understanding the spectral region observed and for various photometric calculations.
PHOTPLAM.relevance_comment: Identifies the effective central wavelength of the filter, which is important for understanding the observed spectral region.

PHOTBW.content: This keyword gives the root mean square (RMS) bandwidth of the filter, indicating the spread or width of the filter's transmission curve. It helps in characterizing the spectral resolution or range of wavelengths captured by the filter.
PHOTBW.relevance_comment: Describes the width of the filter's bandpass, useful for detailed spectral context but less critical than pivot wavelength.

PHOTFNU.content: This keyword provides another measure of inverse sensitivity, specifically the flux density in Janskys that produces one count per second. It's used, along with PHOTFLAM, to convert raw counts into absolute flux units, but in units of flux density per unit frequency.
PHOTFNU.relevance_comment: Provides another critical factor for converting raw data counts into physical flux density per unit frequency.

ORIENTAT.content: This keyword indicates the orientation of the image on the sky, specifically the position angle of the image's Y-axis measured in degrees east of North. It's crucial for correctly orienting images for display or comparison with other data.
ORIENTAT.relevance_comment: Essential for correctly orienting the image on the sky and understanding its spatial layout.

PROPOSID.content: This is the unique identifier for the scientific research project or proposal under which the astronomical data was collected. It allows users to group related observations, understand the scientific context, and find the principal investigators or institutions responsible for the data.
PROPOSID.relevance_comment: This keyword provides crucial context about the scientific program, highly relevant for researchers and interested enthusiasts.

LINENUM_HST.content: This keyword specifies a particular line item or individual observation within a broader HST proposal. While the PROPOSID identifies the overall project, LINENUM_HST helps pinpoint a specific observation request or configuration within that project.
LINENUM_HST.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users to pinpoint specific observations within a proposal, but less critical for general data exploration.

TSTART.content: This keyword indicates the precise moment, in a standardized time system like Barycentric Julian Date, when the astronomical observation officially began. It is fundamental for knowing exactly when the data was collected and for correlating it with other astronomical events.
TSTART.relevance_comment: The start time is essential for nearly all users to understand when the observation occurred.

TSTOP.content: This keyword specifies the precise moment, in a standardized time system, when the astronomical observation concluded. Together with TSTART, it defines the total duration of the observation.
TSTOP.relevance_comment: The stop time is essential for nearly all users to understand the duration and context of the observation.

BJDREFI.content: This keyword stores the integer part of a reference Barycentric Julian Date, which serves as a fixed offset for the timestamps within the data file. It is used in conjunction with BJDREFF to calculate highly precise Barycentric Julian Dates for each data point.
BJDREFI.relevance_comment: This is a technical component for reconstructing precise times, which most end-users won't directly interact with.

BJDREFF.content: This keyword stores the fractional part of a reference Barycentric Julian Date, used alongside BJDREFI to create a complete and extremely precise reference timestamp. Data points' individual times are then given as offsets from this reference.
BJDREFF.relevance_comment: Similar to BJDREFI, this is a technical detail for time reconstruction rather than direct user information.

TIMEDEL.content: This keyword indicates the time interval or cadence between successive data points in the observation, typically in days. It is crucial for understanding the sampling rate of time-series data, such as light curves, and for assessing the temporal resolution.
TIMEDEL.relevance_comment: Highly relevant for anyone analyzing time-series data to understand the sampling frequency.

LIVETIME.content: This keyword specifies the actual accumulated time, in seconds, during which the detector was actively collecting useful photons, excluding any periods of dead time, telemetry saturation, or other interruptions. It is particularly important for calculating accurate fluxes and count rates in high-energy astronomy.
LIVETIME.relevance_comment: Crucial for quantitative analysis (e.g., flux calculations) as it reflects the true integration time.

ONTIME.content: This keyword represents the total duration, in seconds, during which the instrument was observing and collecting valid data, often summing up multiple 'good time intervals'. It is a key metric for understanding the total exposure duration and the reliability of the observation.
ONTIME.relevance_comment: Provides essential context regarding the total data collection time, important for assessing data quality and exposure.

SECTOR.content: For TESS observations, this keyword identifies the specific sector number of the sky that was observed during a particular period. TESS observes the sky in a sequence of sectors, and this number tells you which portion of the sky the data corresponds to.
SECTOR.relevance_comment: Essential for TESS users to understand the spatial context and campaign period of the observation.

CAMERA.content: This keyword identifies which of the TESS mission's four wide-field cameras acquired the data. It's important for understanding the specific instrument responsible for the observation, as each camera covers a distinct region of the sky and may have unique instrumental characteristics.
CAMERA.relevance_comment: Useful for understanding the specific instrument part used and potential instrumental effects on the data.

CCD.content: This specifies the particular Charge-Coupled Device (CCD) within a TESS camera that captured the data. Since each TESS camera has multiple CCDs, this information is valuable for detailed analysis and accounting for any detector-specific nuances or artifacts.
CCD.relevance_comment: Provides specific detector information, useful for detailed analysis but less critical for general users.

TICID.content: This is the unique identifier for the observed star within the TESS Input Catalog (TIC). It is crucial for linking the data to extensive external catalog information, enabling users to retrieve a wealth of stellar properties and cross-reference with other astronomical databases.
TICID.relevance_comment: Essential for uniquely identifying the astronomical target and accessing external catalog data.

KEPLERID.content: This is the unique identifier for the observed star within the Kepler Input Catalog (KIC) for Kepler, or the K2 Ecliptic Plane Input Catalog (EPIC) for K2 data. It's vital for connecting the observation to comprehensive catalog details, allowing users to research stellar properties and cross-match with other datasets.
KEPLERID.relevance_comment: Essential for uniquely identifying the astronomical target and accessing external catalog data.

QUARTER.content: This keyword indicates the specific three-month observing quarter during which the Kepler mission collected the data. It is important for organizing data temporally, understanding the observation epoch, and correlating observations with known mission events or data releases.
QUARTER.relevance_comment: Important for understanding the temporal context of the observation and retrieving related data.

CAMPAIGN.content: This specifies the K2 mission observing campaign number during which the data was acquired. K2 campaigns delineate distinct observing periods and sky fields, making this keyword important for data organization, understanding the observation's spatial and temporal context, and accessing relevant mission documentation.
CAMPAIGN.relevance_comment: Important for understanding the temporal context and specific observing field of the K2 mission.

CROWDSAP.content: This crowding metric represents the ratio of the target's own flux to the total flux measured within the photometric aperture. A value less than 1 indicates that light from nearby stars is contaminating the target's signal, which is critical for assessing the purity and reliability of the photometric measurements.
CROWDSAP.relevance_comment: Crucial for evaluating the quality and purity of photometric measurements, especially for exoplanet light curves.

FLFRCSAP.content: This keyword represents the fraction of the target's total light that falls within the chosen optimal photometric aperture. It's an important indicator of how effectively the target's flux was captured, helping users assess potential aperture losses and overall data quality.
FLFRCSAP.relevance_comment: Important for understanding how much of the target's light was captured in the photometric aperture.

TMAG.content: This is the TESS magnitude of the target star, representing its brightness specifically in the TESS mission's wide red optical bandpass. It is fundamental for characterizing the target's intrinsic luminosity, comparing it with other stars, and performing various astrophysical analyses.
TMAG.relevance_comment: Essential for characterizing the target's brightness and for comparative analysis.

KEPMAG.content: This is the Kepler magnitude of the target star, indicating its brightness in the Kepler mission's broad optical bandpass. It is a critical parameter for characterizing the star, comparing its properties with other stars, and is widely used in stellar and exoplanet research.
KEPMAG.relevance_comment: Essential for characterizing the target's brightness and for comparative analysis.

NUVEXPTI.content: This keyword indicates the exposure time, in seconds, for the Near-Ultraviolet (NUV) channel of the GALEX mission. It directly tells you how long the detector collected light in the NUV band, which is crucial for understanding the signal-to-noise ratio and depth of the observation.
NUVEXPTI.relevance_comment: This is essential information for understanding the quality and depth of an astronomical observation.

FUVEXPTI.content: This keyword specifies the exposure time, in seconds, for the Far-Ultraviolet (FUV) channel of the GALEX mission. Knowing this value is fundamental for assessing how much light was collected in the FUV band and for interpreting the brightness of observed objects in that wavelength range.
FUVEXPTI.relevance_comment: This is essential information for understanding the quality and depth of an astronomical observation.

MESSION.content: This keyword identifies the specific mission phase or survey (e.g., 'GCS' for GALEX Complete Survey) during which the GALEX data was acquired. It provides important context about the overall scientific goals and observation strategy, which can influence data characteristics like depth and coverage.
MESSION.relevance_comment: Provides important context for the observation, useful for researchers and advanced enthusiasts to understand data origins.

ECLESSION.content: This keyword indicates the orbit number during which the GALEX observation was conducted. It can be useful for tracking instrumental performance changes over the mission's lifetime or for correlating observations with specific orbital events.
ECLESSION.relevance_comment: This is useful for advanced users tracking mission specifics or analyzing time-dependent trends, but less critical for general data viewing.

AODO_COR.content: This is a boolean flag indicating whether the aspect-corrected optical distortion has been applied to the GALEX data. This correction is vital for ensuring accurate astrometry (correct positions) and photometry (correct brightness measurements) of objects in the image.
AODO_COR.relevance_comment: This keyword is important for understanding the astrometric and photometric fidelity of the data, relevant for users performing scientific analysis.

CHNLNUM.content: This keyword specifies the channel number (1, 2, 3, or 4) of the Spitzer IRAC instrument used for the observation. Each channel observes a different infrared wavelength, making this essential for understanding the spectral band of the image.
CHNLNUM.relevance_comment: This is essential for understanding the specific wavelength range of the observation, which directly impacts scientific interpretation.

AESSION_ID.content: This is the unique Astronomical Observation Request (AOR) ID for Spitzer data. It links the observation back to its original scientific proposal and planning, allowing users to easily find associated data products and documentation within the Spitzer archive.
AESSION_ID.relevance_comment: This ID is highly useful for researchers to locate related data, proposals, and documentation in archives.

FOVID.content: This keyword is a unique identifier for a specific field of view within a Spitzer observation. It helps distinguish individual frames, especially when an observation includes multiple dither positions or distinct sky regions within a single Astronomical Observation Request.
FOVID.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users dealing with complex observation patterns or needing to link specific frames to their sky positions.

PROJID.content: This keyword identifies the specific Pan-STARRS project or survey (e.g., PS1, PS2) that acquired the data. It provides context about the scientific goals and overall data characteristics of the observation, which can be relevant for understanding data quality and processing.
PROJID.relevance_comment: Provides important context about the specific survey responsible for the data, useful for understanding data characteristics.

SKYCELL.content: This keyword provides the Pan-STARRS sky cell identifier, which is part of a fixed tessellation grid used by the survey to organize and process data across the sky. It helps in precisely locating and retrieving data within the vast Pan-STARRS dataset.
SKYCELL.relevance_comment: This is useful for navigating and organizing data within the large Pan-STARRS survey footprint, particularly for advanced users.

HIERARCH.content: HIERARCH is not a keyword itself, but a FITS convention that allows header keywords to be longer than the standard 8 characters. It enables more descriptive and organized metadata, making headers easier to understand and parse for complex instruments.
HIERARCH.relevance_comment: This is a structural convention that enables other keywords, not a piece of information directly consumed by an end-user.

CHECKSUM.content: This keyword stores a verification code for the entire FITS Header Data Unit (HDU), which includes both the header and the data. Software uses this value to detect if any corruption occurred during data transmission or storage, ensuring the integrity of the file.
CHECKSUM.relevance_comment: This keyword is purely for data integrity verification by software and holds no direct interpretative value for an end-user.

DATASUM.content: This keyword provides a verification code specifically for the data array within an HDU, distinct from the header checksum. It helps ensure that the numerical pixel values themselves have not been corrupted during transfer or storage.
DATASUM.relevance_comment: This keyword serves solely for data integrity verification and has no direct meaning or use for an astronomy app user.

COMPRESS.content: This boolean keyword indicates whether the data within the FITS file was compressed by the instrument on board the spacecraft before transmission. Knowing this can be important for understanding potential effects on data fidelity or the need for decompression.
COMPRESS.relevance_comment: This information is useful for advanced users to understand data processing history and potential fidelity implications.

FPE_SIDE.content: This keyword indicates which of the redundant Focal Plane Electronics (FPE) sides (A or B) was actively used for the MIRI instrument during the observation. This is internal instrument state information, relevant for diagnostics or detailed hardware context.
FPE_SIDE.relevance_comment: This is highly specific instrument hardware status, primarily for engineers or very advanced users diagnosing issues.

ICE_SIDE.content: This keyword specifies which of the redundant Instrument Control Electronics (ICE) sides (A or B) was active for the MIRI instrument. Similar to FPE_SIDE, it provides context about the instrument's operational configuration for specialists.
ICE_SIDE.relevance_comment: This is highly specific instrument hardware status, primarily for engineers or very advanced users diagnosing issues.

TARGCAT.content: This keyword provides a high-level category for the observed target, typically originating from the Astronomer's Proposal Tool (APT). Examples include 'GALAXY', 'STAR', or 'PLANET', giving immediate context to the type of celestial object.
TARGCAT.relevance_comment: This provides immediate context about the observed object type, useful for all user levels.

TARGDESC.content: This keyword offers a more detailed, free-form description of the target, often directly from the Astronomer's Proposal Tool (APT). It can include specific notes, aliases, or a more precise characterization, such as 'Active Galactic Nucleus in M87', enhancing understanding of the observation's scientific goal.
TARGDESC.relevance_comment: This provides valuable descriptive context for the observed target, useful for understanding the science case.

V2_REF.content: This keyword specifies the telescope's V2 coordinate (in arcseconds) of a designated reference point within the image, in the instrument's frame. V2 and V3 coordinates are essential for precise astrometry, allowing scientists to accurately position observations on the sky and align data from different instruments.
V2_REF.relevance_comment: This is fundamental for precise astrometry and WCS transformations, making it highly relevant for analysis but less so for casual viewing.

V3_REF.content: This keyword specifies the telescope's V3 coordinate (in arcseconds) of the same designated reference point. Together with V2_REF, it forms the critical pair of values used to define the precise pointing and orientation of the observation within the telescope's coordinate system.
V3_REF.relevance_comment: This is fundamental for precise astrometry and WCS transformations, making it highly relevant for analysis but less so for casual viewing.

VPARITY.content: This keyword indicates the 'parity' or sense of certain aperture settings, typically represented as 1 or -1. It's an internal instrument configuration detail that helps define how specific optical components or apertures are oriented or behave within the instrument's optical path.
VPARITY.relevance_comment: This keyword describes an internal instrumental detail that is rarely relevant to data interpretation for most end-users, primarily used for internal calibration or instrument modeling.

V3I_YANG.content: This keyword specifies the direction angle of the V3 axis in the instrument's Y-coordinate system, measured in degrees. The V3 axis is part of JWST's unique coordinate system used to define the telescope's orientation relative to its focal plane.
V3I_YANG.relevance_comment: This keyword describes a very specific internal instrument orientation parameter, not typically needed by general users unless performing highly specialized instrument characterization or modeling.

RA_REF.content: This keyword provides the Right Ascension (RA) of the reference point used for the World Coordinate System (WCS) of the image, expressed in degrees. This value, along with DEC_REF, tells you precisely where on the celestial sphere the observed data is located.
RA_REF.relevance_comment: Knowing the celestial coordinates (Right Ascension) of the data is fundamental for any astronomical analysis, display, or comparison with other observations.

DEC_REF.content: This keyword provides the Declination (DEC) of the reference point used for the World Coordinate System (WCS) of the image, expressed in degrees. This value, along with RA_REF, defines the exact position of the observed data on the celestial sphere.
DEC_REF.relevance_comment: Knowing the celestial coordinates (Declination) of the data is fundamental for any astronomical analysis, display, or comparison with other observations.

ROLL_REF.content: This keyword indicates the V3 roll angle at the WCS reference point, measured in degrees (often North over East). It defines the orientation of the instrument's field of view on the sky, telling you how the image is rotated relative to celestial north.
ROLL_REF.relevance_comment: This keyword indicates the orientation of the observation on the sky, which is important for understanding image rotation, aligning with other data, and creating sky-oriented visualizations.

DISPAXIS.content: For spectroscopic data, this keyword indicates the direction along which the spectrum is dispersed on the detector. A value of 1 means dispersion is along the rows, and 2 means along the columns. This is crucial for correctly extracting one-dimensional spectra from two-dimensional detector images.
DISPAXIS.relevance_comment: This keyword is essential for understanding and processing spectroscopic data, as it defines the direction of wavelength dispersion across the detector.

SPECSYS.content: This keyword specifies the spectral reference frame used for any wavelength or velocity information in the data, such as BARYCENT (barycentric), HELIOCEN (heliocentric), or TOPOCENT (topocentric). It is vital for ensuring accurate comparisons of radial velocities or precise wavelength measurements from different observations.
SPECSYS.relevance_comment: This keyword is vital for precise spectroscopic analysis, ensuring that velocity measurements and wavelength scales are correctly referenced and comparable.

VELOSYS.content: This keyword provides the barycentric correction to the radial velocity, measured in meters per second. This value accounts for the observer's (or spacecraft's) motion relative to the Solar System barycenter, allowing observed velocities to be converted to a standard, consistent reference frame.
VELOSYS.relevance_comment: This keyword provides an important velocity correction, crucial for accurate astrophysical measurements of radial velocity and comparing velocities across different datasets.
