REFFRAME.content: This keyword specifies the ephemeris reference frame, such as 'EME2000', used for the precise calculation of the spacecraft's or target's position and velocity. It defines the underlying coordinate system for these orbital mechanics calculations.
REFFRAME.relevance_comment: This keyword specifies the technical coordinate system used for spacecraft ephemeris, which is mostly a backend detail for general users unless performing highly precise astrometry.

EPH_TYPE.content: This keyword indicates the type of ephemeris data used, typically 'Definitive' or 'Predicted'. 'Definitive' means the ephemeris is based on actual tracking data, providing higher accuracy, while 'Predicted' uses models and is less precise. This affects the reliability of pointing and astrometry.
EPH_TYPE.relevance_comment: This keyword provides information about the reliability and precision of the pointing ephemeris, useful for advanced users assessing data quality and positional accuracy.

EPH_TIME.content: This keyword indicates the Modified Julian Date (MJD) at which the recorded position and velocity vectors of the observatory (JWST) are valid. It provides the precise temporal reference point for the accompanying ephemeris data, which is crucial for accurate astrometric and orbital calculations.
EPH_TIME.relevance_comment: This keyword provides the critical time reference for all other ephemeris data.

JWST_X.content: This keyword provides the precise X coordinate of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in a barycentric coordinate system, typically centered on the Solar System barycenter. This information is vital for highly accurate astrometry, parallax corrections, and advanced orbital mechanics calculations for scientific data.
JWST_X.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users performing high-precision astrometry or orbital calculations.

JWST_Y.content: This keyword provides the precise Y coordinate of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in a barycentric coordinate system, typically centered on the Solar System barycenter. This information is vital for highly accurate astrometry, parallax corrections, and advanced orbital mechanics calculations for scientific data.
JWST_Y.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users performing high-precision astrometry or orbital calculations.

JWST_Z.content: This keyword provides the precise Z coordinate of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in a barycentric coordinate system, typically centered on the Solar System barycenter. This information is vital for highly accurate astrometry, parallax corrections, and advanced orbital mechanics calculations for scientific data.
JWST_Z.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users performing high-precision astrometry or orbital calculations.

JWST_DX.content: This keyword specifies the X velocity component of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) within a barycentric coordinate system. This velocity data is crucial for calculating Doppler shifts due to the observatory's motion and for detailed orbital propagation models.
JWST_DX.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users requiring detailed observatory motion for Doppler shift or orbital analyses.

JWST_DY.content: This keyword specifies the Y velocity component of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) within a barycentric coordinate system. This velocity data is crucial for calculating Doppler shifts due to the observatory's motion and for detailed orbital propagation models.
JWST_DY.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users requiring detailed observatory motion for Doppler shift or orbital analyses.

JWST_DZ.content: This keyword specifies the Z velocity component of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) within a barycentric coordinate system. This velocity data is crucial for calculating Doppler shifts due to the observatory's motion and for detailed orbital propagation models.
JWST_DZ.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users requiring detailed observatory motion for Doppler shift or orbital analyses.

OBSGEO-X.content: This keyword indicates the X coordinate of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in a geocentric coordinate system, which places its position relative to the Earth's center. This information is relevant for understanding the telescope's precise location with respect to Earth, which can be important for communication links and specific observation planning.
OBSGEO-X.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users needing the observatory's precise position relative to Earth for specific analyses.

OBSGEO-Y.content: This keyword indicates the Y coordinate of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in a geocentric coordinate system, which places its position relative to the Earth's center. This information is relevant for understanding the telescope's precise location with respect to Earth, which can be important for communication links and specific observation planning.
OBSGEO-Y.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users needing the observatory's precise position relative to Earth for specific analyses.

OBSGEO-Z.content: This keyword indicates the Z coordinate of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in a geocentric coordinate system, which places its position relative to the Earth's center. This information is relevant for understanding the telescope's precise location with respect to Earth, which can be important for communication links and specific observation planning.
OBSGEO-Z.relevance_comment: Useful for advanced users needing the observatory's precise position relative to Earth for specific analyses.

TDB-BEG.content: This keyword specifies the precise start time of the astronomical observation, measured in MJD (Modified Julian Date) using the Barycentric Dynamical Time (TDB) standard. TDB accounts for relativistic effects and the motion of the Earth around the Sun, making it crucial for high-precision timing applications and comparisons across different observatories.
TDB-BEG.relevance_comment: This keyword provides essential timing information, indicating precisely when the observation began, which is fundamental for any astronomical analysis.

TDB-MID.content: This keyword indicates the precise midpoint of the astronomical observation's exposure, measured in MJD (Modified Julian Date) using the Barycentric Dynamical Time (TDB) standard. This is often the most representative single time for an observation, especially when coordinating with other datasets or for tracking rapidly changing phenomena.
TDB-MID.relevance_comment: As the most representative single time for an exposure, this keyword is crucial for accurate temporal alignment and comparison of astronomical data.

TDB-END.content: This keyword specifies the precise end time of the astronomical observation, measured in MJD (Modified Julian Date) using the Barycentric Dynamical Time (TDB) standard. Together with the start time, it defines the exact duration of the exposure, vital for understanding the integration period.
TDB-END.relevance_comment: This keyword provides essential timing information, indicating precisely when the observation ended, which is fundamental for any astronomical analysis.

END.content: This keyword is a structural marker in the FITS header, signaling the absolute end of the current header block. It has no value associated with it and serves purely to delimit the header from the subsequent data section or another header block.
END.relevance_comment: This keyword is purely structural and serves no direct interpretative purpose for an end-user of astronomical data.
